intro:
Holiness (Hebrew qōdeš, from a root meaning “to set apart”; Greek hagiasmos / hagiōsynē) is the most fundamental of God's attributes and the defining quality he demands of his people. In its highest sense holiness belongs to God alone—“Who is like you, majestic in holiness?” (Exodus 15:11); “There is none holy like the LORD” (1 Samuel 2:2). The thrice-repeated cry of the seraphim, “Holy, holy, holy is the LORD of hosts” (Isaiah 6:3), echoed by the living creatures around the throne (Revelation 4:8), makes holiness the supreme category of God's being. At root the word signifies separateness: God is wholly other—transcendent above all creation, and morally pure, utterly removed from sin and corruption (Habakkuk 1:13).

Holiness first confronts humanity as the dangerous nearness of God. The ground at the burning bush is holy (Exodus 3:5); Sinai must be fenced off (Exodus 19:23); and the tabernacle, with its graded zones culminating in the Most Holy Place, teaches that a holy God dwells among an unclean people only through atonement and mediation (Leviticus 16:2). The book of Leviticus, and especially its “Holiness Code,” presses the demand from the sanctuary outward into all of life: “You shall be holy, for I the LORD your God am holy” (Leviticus 19:2; cf. Leviticus 11:44–45). This consecration touched diet, dress, the land, sexual ethics, and the treatment of the poor and the stranger—Israel was to be distinct among the nations as a “kingdom of priests and a holy nation” (Exodus 19:6). The prophets deepened the theme, exposing ritual holiness divorced from justice and pointing to a cleansing only God could give (Isaiah 6:5–7; Ezekiel 36:25–27).

In the New Testament the Holy One of God appears in person: Jesus is so named by the demons who recognize him (Mark 1:24) and by Peter (Acts 3:14), and the Spirit who applies his work is the Holy Spirit. The Levitical imperative is taken up unchanged for the church: “As he who called you is holy, you also be holy in all your conduct, since it is written, ‘You shall be holy, for I am holy’” (1 Peter 1:15–16). Yet the New Testament holds holiness in two tenses. Positionally, every believer is already set apart—the saints (hagioi) are “sanctified in Christ Jesus” (1 Corinthians 1:2) by his atoning blood (Hebrews 10:10). Progressively, that status is to be worked out as the Spirit transforms character over time, “for this is the will of God, your sanctification” (1 Thessalonians 4:3; cf. 2 Corinthians 7:1).

Holiness is therefore not a peripheral virtue but the very goal of redemption: God saves a people in order to share his own character with them and to dwell among them. Its end is communion—the vision of God reserved for the pure in heart (Matthew 5:8)—so that “without holiness no one will see the Lord” (Hebrews 12:14). It is at once a gift secured by grace and a lifelong pursuit, grounding both reverent worship of the God who is utterly other and the moral seriousness of the Christian life, until the redeemed are presented “holy and blameless” before him (Ephesians 5:27).
